Part B.key_evidence: The difference image shows a compact positive residual at the exact candidate position. In the science and template cutouts there appears to be a source at the same fixed location, with the science image brighter than the template rather than clearly offset or streaked. Despite ndethist=2, there is no obvious science-template positional shift to support a mover. Metadata also show a very close PS1 match (distpsnr1=0.47 arcsec) and stellar-like morphology indicators (classtar=0.988, sharpnr=0.185), though sgscore1=0.5 is ambiguous.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: This is most consistent with a real astrophysical variable source, best classified here as a variable_star. The residual is point-like and positive, the candidate seems coincident with a faint persistent counterpart in the template, and the cutouts do not show the offset/dipole behavior expected for a solar-system object. A fixed-position, unresolved source that occasionally brightens is more naturally explained as stellar variability than as a new transient on blank sky.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A plausible alternative is AGN variability if the faint template counterpart is actually a compact galaxy nucleus rather than a star-like source. A supernova is less favored because the alert appears coincident with a persistent source and the object-level detections span about 78 days, which is less typical for a one-off SN with only two coincident detections.
